risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated. Although it notes that "the randomization process has been described in detail [23]," the method is not provided in the text supplied here, so the adequacy of random sequence generation cannot be judged confidently.

Quote: "The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded." Comment: This is the only explicit information about allocation concealment. Although the paper does not name a specific mechanism (e.g., central allocation or sealed opaque envelopes), blinding of the person performing group allocation indicates that the allocator could not foresee the upcoming assignment, so allocation concealment is judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group... There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group." Comment: The trial does not report that participants or treatment personnel were blinded; the control-group dropouts because they preferred the exercise group strongly suggest allocation was known. Because the study outcomes included pain, well-being, disability, and performance tests that could plausibly be influenced by awareness of group assignment, performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "The same physiotherapist, who was blinded to the previous measurement, performed all measurements." Comment: Outcome assessment for the objective/clinician-measured outcomes (range of motion, balance, muscle strength, step-test) was carried out by a physiotherapist who was blinded during measurement, which lowers detection bias. The patient-reported outcomes (CHAQ, CHQ, pain VAS) are subjective, but the paper does not report blinded participants; however, in this study the main assessed outcomes were largely measured by the blinded physiotherapist and the remaining questionnaires are self-reported rather than assessor-rated. Overall, the explicit blinding of the measurer supports low risk for outcome assessment bias.

Quote: "There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group. There were another six dropouts after the first test occasion due to the families’ lack of time." Comment: Attrition was substantial and clearly imbalanced at the randomization stage, with all 10 initial dropouts from the control group. Although some later missingness was due to lack of time, the early imbalance and the fact that participants left because they preferred the exercise group indicate post-randomization loss that could be related to treatment preference and potentially to outcome expectations, which may bias comparisons.
